message.content:
You are verifier #1, independent. Read the ACTUAL code at /Users/[REDACTED]/hc-assay and decide if this is a TRUE production-readiness problem in current code (6 passes hardened). confirmed=true only if real, reproducible, worth fixing (not speculative/by-design).
J-003: Build backend (hatchling) fetched live and un-hashed during `pip install -e .`, defeating the hash-pin/reproducibility guarantee
File: .github/workflows/ci.yml:35
VERIFIED. All three jobs install with `pip install --no-deps -e .` (lines 35, 80, 191). `--no-deps` suppresses runtime deps but does NOT disable PEP 517 build isolation. pyproject.toml declares `build-system.requires = ['hatchling']` and uses hatchling.build (confirmed), and version is dynamic, so pip creates an isolated build env and fetches hatchling + its deps (editables, trove-classifiers) LIVE from PyPI, un-pinned and un-hashed, every CI run. Neither lockfile contains hatchling (`grep -ic '^hatchling==' requirements.lock requirements-core.lock` -> 0/0; 0 matches for any 'hatchling'). Consequences: (1) the workflow's central claim of installing 'EXACTLY the versions pip-audit scanned' is false for the build toolchain; (2) pip-audit (-r requirements.lock) never sees hatchling, so a compromised hatchling/editables patch executes arbitrary code during the editable build yet is never scanned — the exact #F-004 window the comments claim closed; (3) the SBOM omits the build-time dep set; (4) a yanked/changed hatchling makes a green commit non-reproducible.
PoC: grep -ic '^hatchling==' requirements.lock requirements-core.lock -> 0 and 0, while the package still builds via hatchling.build. On a clean runner `pip install --no-deps -e .` emits 'Installing build dependencies: hatchling, editables, ...' resolved live from PyPI with no --require-hashes and no lock entry.
Fix: Include the build backend in the hashed set and disable live build isolation in CI: (a) add hatchling to a build/dev group, regenerate locks with --generate-hashes, and install with `pip install --no-build-isolation --no-deps -e .` so the hashed hatchling is used; or (b) build a wheel in a separately-pinned hashed build env and pip-install that wheel. Extend SBOM/pip-audit coverage to the build toolchain.

attachment.content:
- deep-research: Deep research harness — fan-out web searches, fetch sources, adversarially verify claims, synthesize a cited report. - When the user wants a deep, multi-source, fact-checked research report on any topic. BEFORE invoking, check if the question is specific enough to research directly — if underspecified (e.g., "what car to buy" without budget/use-case/region), ask 2-3 clarifying questions to narrow scope. Then pass the refined question as args, weaving the answers in.
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- verify: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by running the app and observing behavior. Use when asked to verify a PR, confirm a fix works, test a change manually, check that a feature works, or validate local changes before pushing.
- code-review: Review the current diff for correctness bugs and reuse/simplification/efficiency cleanups at the given effort level (low/medium: fewer, high-confidence findings; high→max: broader coverage, may include uncertain findings; ultra: deep multi-agent review in the cloud). Pass --comment to post findings as inline PR comments, or --fix to apply the findings to the working tree after the review.
- simplify: Review the changed code for reuse, simplification, efficiency, and altitude cleanups, then apply the fixes. Quality only — it does not hunt for bugs; use /code-review for that.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled cloud agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring cloud agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Reference for the Claude API / Anthropic SDK — model ids, pricing, params, streaming, tool use, MCP, agents, caching, token counting, model migration.
TRIGGER — read BEFORE opening the target file; don't skip because it "looks like a one-liner" — whenever: the prompt names Claude/Anthropic in any form (Claude, Anthropic, Fable, Opus, Sonnet, Haiku, `anthropic`, `@anthropic-ai`, `claude-*`, `us.anthropic.*`, `[1m]`); the user asks about an LLM (pricing/model choice/limits/caching) — never answer from memory; OR the task is LLM-shaped with provider unstated (agent/MCP/tool-definition/multi-agent/RAG/LLM-judge/computer-use; generate/summarize/extract/classify/rewrite/converse over NL; debugging refusals/cutoffs/streaming/tool-calls/tokens).
SKIP only when another provider is being worked on (overrides all triggers): OpenAI/GPT/Gemini/Llama/Mistral/Cohere/Ollama named in the query; OR `grep -rE 'openai|langchain_openai|google.generativeai|genai|mistralai|cohere|ollama'` over the project hits (run this grep FIRST if no provider named — don't Read the file).
- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a pull request
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

content.text: I'll verify this finding by examining the actual code.

input.command: cd /Users/[REDACTED]/hc-assay && grep -n 'build-system\|requires\|build-backend\|dynamic' pyproject.toml; echo "---LOCKS---"; grep -ic 'hatchling' requirements.lock requirements-core.lock 2>/dev/null

content.content:
with:
          python-version: ${{ matrix.python-version }}
      - name: Install (no extras, hash-pinned)
        run: |
          # Install the dev toolchain from the hashed, audited core lockfile so tests run against
          # EXACTLY the versions pip-audit scanned and the SBOM describes — not a live PyPI
          # resolution where a malicious patch release could execute under test (#F-004). The
          # core lock carries only the `dev` extra (no backends), preserving the ADR-0006 guard.
          python -m pip install --upgrade pip
          pip install --require-hashes -r requirements-core.lock
          pip install --no-deps -e .
      - name: Lint (ruff)
        run: ruff check src tests
      - name: Format check (ruff)
        run: ruff format --check src tests
      - name: Type-check (mypy)
        run: mypy
      - name: Test (pytest)
        run: pytest -q

  # Extras lane: install the real backends and run the integration suite so the backend code
          # Install from the hashed all-extras lockfile so the integration suite executes against
          # exactly the audited, pinned versions — closing the window where a compromised patch
          # release within a pyproject range could run during the real-backend tests (#F-004).
          python -m pip install --upgrade pip
          pip install --require-hashes -r requirements.lock
          pip install --no-deps -e .
      - name: Type-check against real backend types (mypy)
        run: mypy
      - name: Verify service containers are reachable (no silent skip)
        # #H-009: the live Postgres/Qdrant tests SKIP when the service is unreachable, so a
        # misconfigured/never-started container would let the integration job report GREEN without
          set -euo pipefail
          # Fail if any dependency carries a strong-copyleft license incompatible with the
          # Apache-2.0 distribution (#147). Scan the SAME hash-pinned set pip-audit audited, not a
          # live `pip install -e .[all]` resolution that could pull different versions than the
          # lockfile pins (#F-013). Advisory monitoring of the rest is handled by Dependabot.
          pip install --require-hashes -r requirements.lock
          pip install --no-deps -e .
          python -m pip install pip-licenses==5.5.5  # pinned for reproducible license output (#G-022)
          pip-licenses --format=json --with-system > licenses.json
          python scripts/license_gate.py licenses.json
      - name: Upload license report

content.text:
Confirmed. The finding is accurate in all material respects:

- `build-system.requires = ["hatchling"]` with `build-backend = "hatchling.build"` and `dynamic = ["version"]` — so `pip install -e .` triggers PEP 517 build isolation, fetching hatchling (+ editables, trove-classifiers) live from PyPI.
- All three jobs use `pip install --no-deps -e .` (lines 35, 80, 191). `--no-deps` does NOT disable build isolation; only `--no-build-isolation` would.
- Neither lockfile contains hatchling (0/0 matches).
- No `--no-build-isolation` flag anywhere in CI; no separate hashed wheel build.

The contradiction with the workflow's own comments is real: they claim installation of "EXACTLY the versions pip-audit scanned" and that the #F-004 arbitrary-code-execution window is closed, but the build backend runs un-pinned, un-hashed, un-audited code during the editable build on every CI run.

Severity assessment: This is the build-time supply-chain analogue of #F-004 — a window the project explicitly claims to have closed. A compromised hatchling/editables patch executes arbitrary code in CI, and it's invisible to pip-audit and the SBOM. It also breaks reproducibility (yanked/changed hatchling => non-deterministic green). However, it's build-tooling rather than the shipped runtime artifact, exploitation requires a PyPI-side compromise of a maintained package, and the impact is on CI rather than production runtime — placing it at high rather than critical.

input.reasoning: Verified against actual code. pyproject.toml lines 1-3,7 declare build-system.requires=["hatchling"], build-backend="hatchling.build", and dynamic=["version"], so an editable install triggers PEP 517 build isolation that fetches hatchling + transitive build deps (editables, trove-classifiers) live from PyPI. All three CI jobs (.github/workflows/ci.yml lines 35, 80, 191) use `pip install --no-deps -e .`; `--no-deps` suppresses runtime deps but does NOT disable build isolation — only `--no-build-isolation` would. grep confirms zero hatchling entries in requirements.lock and requirements-core.lock, and there is no `--no-build-isolation` flag or separate hashed-wheel build anywhere in CI. Therefore the build toolchain is fetched un-pinned, un-hashed, and unseen by pip-audit (which scans only the lockfiles) and the SBOM. This directly contradicts the workflow's own comments claiming installs use 'EXACTLY the versions pip-audit scanned' and that the #F-004 arbitrary-code-execution window is closed: a compromised hatchling/editables release would execute arbitrary code during the editable build on every run, undetected. Reproducibility is also broken (yanked/changed build backend => non-deterministic green). Not by-design and not speculative — it is a concrete gap in the project's stated hash-pin/reproducibility guarantee. Severity high rather than critical because it affects the CI build toolchain, not the shipped runtime artifact, and exploitation requires an upstream PyPI compromise of a maintained package.

content.text: Confirmed J-003 as a true high-severity finding. The build backend (hatchling) is fetched live and un-hashed during `pip install --no-deps -e .` in all three CI jobs, defeating the hash-pin/reproducibility guarantee the workflow explicitly claims to enforce. Details are in the StructuredOutput call.
